title: The Street Musician
summary: A narrator stops to watch a street musician playing outside a busy train station, reflecting on how music cuts through the noise of everyday life and wondering about the musician's story.
body: I had been walking too fast for too long that morning, the way you do in a city when you have somewhere to be and the crowd seems to be working against you. Then I heard it — a cello, of all things, being played outside the entrance to the station. I almost walked past. I nearly always do. But something about the sound made me slow down, and then stop altogether. The man playing it was perhaps fifty years old, with grey at his temples and a worn leather case open at his feet, a few coins already scattered inside. He was playing something I recognised but could not name — slow and searching, like someone thinking out loud. The morning commuters streamed past him on both sides, their faces fixed forward, headphones in, coffees in hand. Very few of them turned their heads. This is one of the things about cities that I find both fascinating and a little sad: the extraordinary can exist right beside the ordinary, and most people are too busy to notice. I stood and watched him for about ten minutes. In that time, three people stopped. A woman in her thirties who paused, closed her eyes for a moment, and then walked on. A child who tugged at her father's sleeve until he knelt down and put a coin in the case. And an older gentleman who stood listening with his hands clasped behind his back, as if he were attending a concert hall. I found myself wondering about the musician. Had he trained at a conservatoire somewhere, spent years mastering the instrument, and ended up here? Or had this always been his preferred stage — the open air, the unpredictable audience, the coins and the cold? There was no embarrassment in how he played. No apology in his posture. He was not performing for the crowd; he seemed to be performing for himself, and for whoever happened to need it. I thought about how much invisible labour there is in a city — the work of making things beautiful, or bearable, or meaningful, that rarely gets acknowledged. The street musician does not appear in any schedule. He is not employed by the city or reviewed by a critic. He just plays. And for ten minutes on a grey Tuesday morning, that was more than enough.

annotations.meaningVi: cản trở bạn, chống lại bạn
annotations.exampleEn: When things seem to be working against you, try to stay calm.

annotations.meaningVi: trong tất cả mọi thứ (nhấn mạnh sự bất ngờ)
annotations.exampleEn: She became a pilot, of all things, after growing up afraid of heights.
The music made him slow down and stop altogether.
He was a distinguished man with grey at his temples.
The violin was stored in a worn leather case.
The melody had a searching, melancholy quality.

annotations.meaningVi: nói to suy nghĩ, diễn đạt suy nghĩ thành tiếng
annotations.exampleEn: Sorry, I was just thinking out loud — ignore me.

annotations.meaningVi: ùa qua, đổ qua như dòng chảy
annotations.exampleEn: Commuters streamed past the entrance without stopping.

annotations.meaningVi: nhìn thẳng về phía trước, mặt hướng thẳng
annotations.exampleEn: She walked with her gaze fixed forward, ignoring the noise.
He stood with his hands clasped behind his back, deep in thought.
She studied violin at a prestigious conservatoire in Paris.
Mastering the instrument takes years of dedicated practice.
For her, the park bench was her preferred stage for practising.

annotations.meaningVi: lao động vô hình, công việc không được thấy/nhận ra
annotations.exampleEn: Caring work is often described as invisible labour.
The music made the long wait feel bearable.

annotations.meaningVi: được ghi nhận, được công nhận
annotations.exampleEn: Her contribution was never formally acknowledged.

annotations.meaningVi: hơn là đủ, quá đủ rồi
annotations.exampleEn: For a Tuesday morning, that performance was more than enough.

grammarNotes.title: Past Perfect Continuous — Extended background actions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu mở đầu 'I had been walking too fast for too long' dùng thì quá khứ hoàn thành tiếp diễn (had been + V-ing) để diễn tả hành động đang tiếp diễn trong một khoảng thời gian trước một sự kiện quá khứ khác. Khác với quá khứ đơn ('I walked fast'), thì này nhấn mạnh tính liên tục và khoảng thời gian của hành động.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The opening sentence 'I had been walking too fast for too long' uses the past perfect continuous (had been + V-ing) to show an ongoing action over a period leading up to a past event. Unlike the past simple ('I walked fast'), this tense emphasises the duration and continuity of the action.

grammarNotes.title: Relative Clauses — Adding detail without extra sentences
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài sử dụng mệnh đề quan hệ để thêm thông tin mà không cần câu riêng. 'A woman in her thirties who paused, closed her eyes for a moment, and then walked on' — mệnh đề 'who paused...' bổ nghĩa cho 'a woman'. 'The work of making things beautiful, or bearable, or meaningful, that rarely gets acknowledged' — 'that rarely gets acknowledged' làm rõ 'the work'. Mệnh đề quan hệ giúp văn viết trở nên liền mạch hơn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses relative clauses to add information without separate sentences. 'A woman in her thirties who paused, closed her eyes for a moment, and then walked on' — 'who paused…' modifies 'a woman'. 'The work of making things beautiful, or bearable, or meaningful, that rarely gets acknowledged' — 'that rarely gets acknowledged' defines 'the work'. Relative clauses create cohesion in extended writing.

translationVi:
Tôi đã đi quá nhanh quá lâu sáng hôm đó, theo cái cách bạn đi trong một thành phố khi bạn có nơi phải đến và đám đông dường như đang cản trở bạn. Rồi tôi nghe thấy — một cây đàn cello, trong tất cả mọi thứ, đang được chơi bên ngoài lối vào ga tàu. Tôi gần như đi thẳng qua. Tôi gần như vẫn luôn làm vậy. Nhưng có gì đó trong âm thanh khiến tôi bước chậm lại, rồi dừng hẳn.

Người đàn ông chơi đàn có lẽ năm mươi tuổi, tóc mai đã bạc và hộp đàn da cũ kỹ mở ra dưới chân, vài đồng xu đã được ném vào trong. Ông đang chơi thứ gì đó tôi nhận ra nhưng không thể đặt tên — chậm rãi và như đang tìm kiếm, như ai đó đang suy nghĩ thành tiếng.

Những người đi làm sáng sớm đổ qua ông ở hai bên, mặt hướng thẳng về phía trước, tai nghe đã đeo, tay cầm cà phê. Rất ít trong số họ quay đầu lại. Đây là một trong những điều về các thành phố mà tôi thấy vừa thú vị vừa đôi chút buồn: điều phi thường có thể tồn tại ngay bên cạnh điều bình thường, và hầu hết mọi người quá bận rộn để nhận ra.

Tôi đứng và nhìn ông khoảng mười phút. Trong thời gian đó, ba người dừng lại. Một người phụ nữ khoảng ba mươi tuổi dừng lại, nhắm mắt lại một lúc, rồi đi tiếp. Một đứa trẻ kéo tay áo cha cho đến khi người cha quỳ xuống và bỏ một đồng xu vào hộp đàn. Và một quý ông lớn tuổi đứng nghe với hai tay khoanh sau lưng, như thể ông đang dự một buổi hòa nhạc.

Tôi thấy mình tự hỏi về người nhạc sĩ. Ông có từng học tại một nhạc viện nào đó, dành nhiều năm thành thạo nhạc cụ, rồi kết thúc ở đây không? Hay đây luôn là sân khấu ông ưa thích — không khí mở, khán giả bất định, những đồng xu và cái lạnh?

Không có gì là ngượng ngùng trong cách ông chơi. Không có gì xin lỗi trong tư thế của ông. Ông không biểu diễn cho đám đông; ông dường như đang biểu diễn cho chính mình, và cho bất kỳ ai tình cờ cần đến điều đó.

Tôi nghĩ về bao nhiêu lao động vô hình có trong một thành phố — công việc làm cho mọi thứ trở nên đẹp, hoặc có thể chịu đựng được, hoặc có ý nghĩa, mà hiếm khi được ghi nhận. Người nhạc sĩ đường phố không xuất hiện trong bất kỳ lịch trình nào. Ông không được thành phố thuê hay nhà phê bình đánh giá. Ông chỉ chơi thôi. Và trong mười phút vào một sáng thứ Ba xám xịt đó, điều đó đã hơn là đủ.

questions.prompt: What instrument was the street musician playing?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'a cello, of all things, being played outside the entrance to the station.'
questions.hint: The narrator uses the phrase 'of all things', suggesting it was unexpected.

questions.prompt: How does the narrator describe the music the musician was playing?
options: Loud and triumphant, filling the station entrance
Slow and searching, like someone thinking out loud
Light and cheerful, brightening the grey morning
Familiar and repetitive, from a well-known classical piece
questions.explanation: The narrator describes it as 'slow and searching, like someone thinking out loud.'
questions.hint: The narrator uses a simile to describe the quality of the music.

questions.prompt: How did most of the morning commuters respond to the musician?
options: They stopped briefly and smiled before continuing
They walked past without turning their heads
They gathered in a small crowd to listen
They asked the musician to play quieter music
questions.explanation: The narrator says commuters had 'their faces fixed forward, headphones in, coffees in hand. Very few of them turned their heads.'
questions.hint: Think about what 'faces fixed forward' and 'headphones in' suggest.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator find 'both fascinating and a little sad' about cities?
options: The way musicians are forced to play outdoors for money
The fact that most people commute alone every day
That the extraordinary can exist beside the ordinary and most people are too busy to notice
How quickly people forget the music they hear on their commute
questions.explanation: The narrator says: 'the extraordinary can exist right beside the ordinary, and most people are too busy to notice.'
questions.hint: The narrator makes a broader observation about city life.

questions.prompt: How did the child cause a coin to end up in the musician's case?
options: The child ran up to the case and dropped the coin in independently
The child tugged at their father's sleeve until he knelt down and placed a coin
The child asked the musician for permission before donating
The child was handed a coin by the narrator to put in the case
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'A child who tugged at her father's sleeve until he knelt down and put a coin in the case.'
questions.hint: The child's action involved the father.

questions.prompt: What did the older gentleman's posture suggest about how he was listening?
options: He was uncomfortable and wanted to move on
He was listening very formally, as if attending a concert hall
He was checking his phone while listening
He was trying to identify the piece of music
questions.explanation: The narrator says he stood 'as if he were attending a concert hall,' suggesting formal, attentive listening.
questions.hint: His posture — hands clasped behind his back — is described with a comparison.

questions.prompt: What two possible life histories does the narrator imagine for the musician?
options: That he played in an orchestra or that he was self-taught
That he trained at a conservatoire or that the street was always his preferred stage
That he was a teacher or that he had recently retired from performing
That he was homeless or that he chose busking for the freedom it offered
questions.explanation: The narrator wonders: 'Had he trained at a conservatoire somewhere… and ended up here? Or had this always been his preferred stage?'
questions.hint: The narrator poses two specific questions about the musician's past.

questions.prompt: Who does the narrator say the musician seemed to be performing for?
options: For the commuters who walked past
For the child who put a coin in the case
For himself, and for whoever happened to need it
For the older gentleman who stood and listened
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'He seemed to be performing for himself, and for whoever happened to need it.'
questions.hint: The narrator contrasts this with performing for the crowd.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator mean by 'invisible labour'?
options: Work done by people who are not officially registered as workers
The physical effort of carrying heavy instruments through the city
Work that makes things beautiful or meaningful but is rarely acknowledged
Jobs that are done at night when nobody can see them
questions.explanation: The narrator defines it as 'the work of making things beautiful, or bearable, or meaningful, that rarely gets acknowledged.'
questions.hint: The narrator explains exactly what they mean by the phrase.

questions.prompt: What day of the week did the narrator observe the street musician?
questions.explanation: The narrator says: 'for ten minutes on a grey Tuesday morning, that was more than enough.'
questions.hint: This detail appears in the final sentence.

cloze.template: I had been walking too fast for too long that morning, the way you do in a city when you have somewhere to be and the crowd seems to be working {{b1}} you. Then I heard it — a cello, of all things, being played outside the entrance to the station. I almost walked past. I nearly always do. But something about the sound made me slow down, and then stop altogether. The man playing it was perhaps fifty years old, with grey at his {{b2}} and a worn leather case open at his feet, a few coins already scattered inside. He was playing something I recognised but could not name — slow and {{b3}}, like someone thinking out loud. The morning commuters {{b4}} past him on both sides, their faces fixed forward, headphones in, coffees in hand. Very few of them turned their heads. This is one of the things about cities that I find both fascinating and a little sad: the extraordinary can exist right beside the ordinary, and most people are too busy to notice. I stood and watched him for about ten minutes. In that time, three people stopped. A woman in her thirties who paused, closed her eyes for a moment, and then walked on. A child who tugged at her father's sleeve until he knelt down and put a coin in the case. And an older gentleman who stood listening with his hands clasped behind his back, as if he were attending a {{b5}}. I found myself wondering about the musician. Had he trained at a {{b6}} somewhere, spent years mastering the instrument, and ended up here? Or had this always been his preferred stage — the open air, the unpredictable audience, the coins and the cold? There was no embarrassment in how he played. No apology in his posture. He was not performing for the crowd; he seemed to be performing for himself, and for whoever happened to need it. I thought about how much {{b7}} there is in a city — the work of making things beautiful, or {{b8}}, or meaningful, that rarely gets {{b9}}. The street musician does not appear in any schedule. He is not employed by the city or reviewed by a critic. He just plays. And for ten minutes on a grey Tuesday morning, that was more than {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: 'Working against you' is a fixed idiom meaning that circumstances are creating obstacles or opposition.
'Grey at his temples' refers to grey hair at the sides of the forehead — a common way to describe a middle-aged person.
'Searching' describes music that seems to be looking for something — reflective and exploratory in quality.
'Streamed past' — 'stream' suggests a continuous, flowing movement of many people, like a stream of water.
'Concert hall' completes the simile — the gentleman's posture suggested he was treating the street music with the same respect as a formal concert.
A 'conservatoire' is a specialist music college — it suggests a high level of formal training.
'Invisible labour' is the exact phrase — it refers to work that exists but goes unrecognised.
'Bearable' means endurable — the music does not make the commute wonderful, but it makes it possible to endure.
'Acknowledged' means formally recognised or given credit — the narrator is noting that this work goes unappreciated.
'More than enough' is the idiom — it emphasises that the experience exceeded what was needed, even in its simplicity.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator describes 'invisible labour' — the work of making daily life more beautiful or bearable that rarely gets acknowledged. What other forms of invisible labour exist in cities or in society? Why do we tend to overlook contributions that don't come with a formal title or a salary?
